skill.name: swiftui-expert-skill
skill.description: Write, review, or improve SwiftUI code following best practices for state management, view composition, performance, modern APIs, and iOS 26+ Liquid Glass adoption.
skill.summary: Expert guidance for building modern SwiftUI applications with correct state management, optimal performance patterns, and Apple design guidelines.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer produced 226 false positive findings due to pattern matching on markdown documentation content. External command patterns matched file path references like 'references/state-management.md' and Swift code examples, not actual shell execution. Network finding was a GitHub source URL in YAML frontmatter. All blocker findings for cryptographic algorithms and system reconnaissance are incorrect pattern matches on documentation text. No executable code or security risks present.

content.user_title: Build Modern SwiftUI Apps with Expert Best Practices
content.value_statement: SwiftUI developers struggle with state management complexity and deprecated API patterns. This skill provides fact-based guidance for modern SwiftUI development using @Observable, correct view composition, and iOS 26+ Liquid Glass styling.
actual_capabilities: Review SwiftUI code for correct property wrapper usage (@State, @Binding, @Observable)
Identify and replace deprecated APIs with modern equivalents
Optimize view composition for performance and diffing efficiency
Implement iOS 26+ Liquid Glass styling with proper fallbacks
Validate ForEach identity patterns and list performance
Ensure thread safety with @MainActor and modern Swift concurrency
limitations: Does not enforce specific architecture patterns like MVVM or VIPER
Cannot execute or compile Swift code - provides guidance only
Liquid Glass patterns require iOS 26+ or Xcode previews
Does not replace official Apple documentation or WWDC sessions

use_cases.title: iOS Developer Building New Features
use_cases.description: Use when creating new SwiftUI views to ensure correct state management with @Observable, modern API usage like foregroundStyle() and NavigationStack, and optimal view structure from the start.

use_cases.title: Code Review for Existing SwiftUI Projects
use_cases.description: Use when auditing legacy SwiftUI code to identify deprecated APIs, incorrect property wrappers, performance anti-patterns, and opportunities for modernization.

use_cases.title: Learning Modern SwiftUI Patterns
use_cases.description: Use when transitioning from UIKit or older SwiftUI to learn current best practices for data flow, view composition, and Apple design guidelines.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Code Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SwiftUI view for correct state management and modern API usage. Check property wrapper selection, deprecated APIs, and view composition patterns.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a SwiftUI view file and want to ensure it follows current best practices.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Legacy Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this SwiftUI code to use modern APIs. Replace ObservableObject with @Observable, update deprecated modifiers, and optimize the view structure for performance.
prompt_templates.scenario: You inherited older SwiftUI code using pre-iOS 17 patterns and need to modernize it.

prompt_templates.title: Implement New Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me build a new SwiftUI feature with correct data flow. Design the state management using @Observable, structure views for optimal diffing, and apply iOS 26+ styling where appropriate.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new feature and want to build it correctly from the beginning.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Performance Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this SwiftUI view for performance problems. Identify unnecessary state updates, view identity issues in ForEach, hot path optimizations, and layout thrashing causes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Your SwiftUI view is causing unexpected re-renders or feels sluggish during interaction.

output_examples.input: Review this view: struct ProductView: View { @StateObject var vm: ProductViewModel; var body: some View { Text(vm.title) } }
output_examples.output: Issues found: (1) Using @StateObject with ObservableObject - prefer @Observable class with @State for new code. (2) StateObject should be private. (3) Consider passing title as let parameter if read-only. Recommended: Use @Observable class, mark @State as private, pass read-only values directly.

output_examples.input: How do I handle navigation to a detail view when tapping a list row?
output_examples.output: Use NavigationStack with navigationDestination for type-safe navigation. Replace NavigationView with NavigationStack at root. Use NavigationLink with value parameter and navigationDestination(for:) modifier. This provides compile-time safety and proper state management compared to older NavigationView patterns.

best_practices: Prefer @Observable over ObservableObject for new SwiftUI code with @MainActor for thread safety
Keep view body simple and pure - extract complex logic to models and subviews
Use stable identity in ForEach with unique identifiers, never .indices for dynamic content
anti_patterns: Declaring passed values as @State or @StateObject instead of using @Binding or let parameters
Using GeometryReader when containerRelativeFrame or visualEffect alternatives exist
Creating objects or performing heavy computation directly in view body

faq.question: Should I use @Observable or ObservableObject for new SwiftUI projects?
faq.answer: Use @Observable for all new code. It provides automatic observation without @Published, works with nested objects, and integrates with Swift concurrency. Only use ObservableObject for maintaining legacy code.

faq.question: When should I use @Binding versus passing a value directly?
faq.answer: Use @Binding when the child view needs to modify the parent's state. Use let for read-only values. If you need reactivity to changes but not modification, use var with .onChange() modifier.

faq.question: Is Liquid Glass styling required for iOS apps?
faq.answer: No, Liquid Glass is only available on iOS 26+ and should only be adopted when explicitly requested. This skill provides fallback patterns using .ultraThinMaterial for earlier iOS versions.

faq.question: Why does my ForEach cause crashes with dynamic arrays?
faq.answer: Using .indices as identity fails when array order changes. Always use stable unique identifiers like id property. For dynamic content, ensure each element has a consistent, unique identity across updates.

faq.question: How do I debug unexpected view re-renders in SwiftUI?
faq.answer: Add Self._printChanges() inside your view body to log what triggered updates. Common causes include passing large config objects, redundant state assignments, and dependencies on frequently-changing values.

faq.question: Can this skill enforce a specific architecture like MVVM?
faq.answer: No, this skill focuses on facts and best practices rather than architectural opinions. It encourages separating business logic for testability but does not enforce specific patterns like MVVM or VIPER.
